perturbation.original_text: 12.3 This Agreement shall be construed, enforced, performed and in all respects governed by and in accordance with the laws in the State of Washington. In any action or suit to enforce any right or remedy under this Agreement the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.changed_text:
12.11 This Agreement, along with the Ancillary Agreements, together contain the entire agreement of the parties with respect to the premises, and may not be modified or amended except by a written instrument executed by the party sought to be charged or bound thereby.

12.3 This Agreement shall be construed, enforced, performed and in all respects governed by and in accordance with the laws in the State of California. In any action or suit to enforce any right or remedy under this Agreement the prevailing party shall NOT be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law clause has been changed to California and the attorney's fees provision has been negated and moved to a different section, which could cause confusion about whether Washington or California law applies. The removal of the attorney's fees clause would make it hard to enforce certain legal claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP ¬І 1021 generally allows parties to contract regarding attorney's fees. By stating in the contract that the prevailing party will NOT be entitled to recover attorney's fees, it contradicts what would normally be allowed under the default rules of California law, where fee-shifting agreements are commonly enforced.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorneyвАЩs fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from Washington to California and removes the provision for attorney's fees to the prevailing party. CCP ¬І 1021, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that "Except as attorneyвАЩs fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties". By explicitly stating that the prevailing party will NOT be entitled to recover attorney's fees, the changed text directly contradicts the ability for parties to agree on attorney's fees, which is generally allowed under California law and is a structural flaw.

perturbation.original_text: 12.2 Any notice required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be made in writing and shall be deemed to have been given or made if it is in writing and is: (i) delivered in person, (ii) sent by same day or overnight courier, (iii) mailed by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, addressed to the party at its address set forth below or at such other address as such party may subsequently furnish to the other party by notice hereunder, or (iv) delivered by facsimile, the transmittal of which shall be confirmed by a telephone call to the other party and by dispatch of a confirming copy of the transmittal by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid. Notices will be deemed effective on the date of delivery in the case of personal delivery, or three (3) business days after mailing, or on the date of dispatch in the case of notification by facsimile (assuming confirmation of transmission). The parties' addresses for purposes of notice shall be as set forth above, provided that all notices to Inktomi shall be sent to the
perturbation.changed_text: 12.2 Any notice required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be made orally. Notices will be deemed effective when the party giving the notice deems it effective. The parties' addresses for purposes of communication shall be as set forth above, provided that all notices to Inktomi shall be sent to the
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the written notice requirement, which is standard and expected in commercial contracts, and makes it easier to claim that a notice was never received or was unclear. It also removes the specific methods by which notice must be transmitted, and when the notice will be effective, causing problems with establishing the validity of the notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly mandating *written* notice in all scenarios, UCC ¬І 1-202(d) states that 'A person 'notifies' or 'gives' a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it'. Removing any specific reasonable steps like 'written notice' and allowing the party to determine when a notice is effective would cause uncertainty and be likely deemed an unreasonable method of notice under the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ¬І 1-202. Notice; Knowledge. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! √Ч No thank you ¬І 1-202. Notice; Knowledge. (a) Subject to subsection (f), a person has " notice " of a fact if the person: (1) has actual knowledge of it; (2) has received a notice or notification of it; or (3) from all the facts and circumstances known to the person at the time in question, has reason to know that it exists. (b) " Knowledge " means actual knowledge. "Knows" has a corresponding meaning. (c) " Discover ", " learn ", or words of similar import refer to knowledge rather than to reason to know. (d) A person " notifies " or " gives " a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it. (e) Subject to subsection (f), a person " receives " a notice or notification when: (1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances at the place of business through which the contract was made or at another location held out by that person as the place for receipt of such communications. (f) Notice, knowledge, or a notice or notification received by an organization is effective for a particular transaction from the time it is brought to the attention of the individual conducting that transaction and, in any event, from the time it would have been brought to the individual's attention if the organization had exercised due diligence. An organization exercises due diligence if it maintains reasonable routines for communicating significant information to the person conducting the transaction and there is reasonable compliance with the routines. Due diligence does not require an individual acting for the organization to communicate information unless the communication is part of the individual's regular duties or the individual has reason to k
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the requirement for written notice, replacing it with oral notice, and allows the notifying party to determine when the notice is effective. This contradicts UCC ¬І 1-202(d), which states that notice must be given by 'taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course'. Removing the written requirement and allowing the notifying party to determine effectiveness introduces significant uncertainty and deviates from reasonable steps, making it easier to dispute whether a notice was actually given or received. The original text specified methods to ensure reasonable notice, but the changed text eliminates this, creating a clear conflict with the intent of UCC ¬І 1-202.

perturbation.original_text: 12.5 Except as expressly permitted hereunder or in Exhibit F hereto, neither party may transfer, assign or sublicense this Agreement, or any rights or obligations hereunder, whether by contract or by operation of law, except with the express written consent of the other party, and any attempted transfer, assignment or sublicense by a party in violation of this Section shall be void. For purposes of this Agreement, an "transfer" under this Section shall be deemed to include, without limitation, the following: (a) a merger or any other combination of an entity with another party (other than a reincorporation of Inktomi from the State of California to the State of Delaware), whether or not the entity is the surviving entity; (b) any transaction or series of transactions whereby a third party acquires direct or indirect power to control the management and policies of an entity, whether through the acquisition of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise; (c) in the case of Inktomi, the sale or other transfer of Inktomi's search engine business or any other substantial portion of Inktomi's assets (whether in a single transaction or series of transactions), or (d) the transfer of any rights or obligations in the course of a liquidation or other similar reorganization of an entity (other than a reincorporation of Inktomi from the State of California to the State of Delaware). Neither party will unreasonably withhold or delay its consent to a requested transfer, assignment or sublicense. Subject to the provisions of this Section, this Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of each party and their respective successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.5 Except as expressly permitted hereunder or in Exhibit F hereto, either party may transfer, assign or sublicense this Agreement, or any rights or obligations hereunder, whether by contract or by operation of law, except with the express written consent of the assigning party, and any attempted transfer, assignment or sublicense by a party without informing the counterparty shall be valid. For purposes of this Agreement, an "transfer" under this Section shall be deemed to include, without limitation, the following: (a) a merger or any other combination of an entity with another party (other than a reincorporation of Inktomi from the State of California to the State of Delaware), whether or not the entity is the surviving entity; (b) any transaction or series of transactions whereby a third party acquires direct or indirect power to control the management and policies of an entity, whether through the acquisition of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise; (c) in the case of Inktomi, the sale or other transfer of Inktomi's search engine business or any other substantial portion of Inktomi's assets (whether in a single transaction or series of transactions), or (d) the transfer of any rights or obligations in the course of a liquidation or other similar reorganization of an entity (other than a reincorporation of Inktomi from the State of California to the State of Delaware). The transferring party will delay informing the counterparty of its consent to a requested transfer, assignment or sublicense for as long as possible. Subject to the provisions of this Section, this Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of each party and their respective successors and assigns.
perturbation.explanation: The modification allows either party to unilaterally transfer, assign, or sublicense the agreement, provided that the other party consents to this transfer - but the consenting party is the assigning party. Also, the transfer, assignment or sublicense by a party without informing the counterparty shall be valid. It also states the transferring party will delay informing the counterparty of its consent to a requested transfer for as long as possible. This completely reverses the standard non-assignment clause and potentially harms the counterparty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restatement (Second) of Contracts ¬І 317. Assignment of a Right
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Restatement allows for assignment of rights, it also considers the validity and effects of contractual limitations on assignment. By essentially nullifying the requirement for consent and allowing assignment without notice, the altered clause significantly undermines the protections that a standard non-assignment clause provides. This creates a situation where one party could transfer their obligations to an unsuitable third party without the other party's knowledge or consent, contrary to the implicit understanding in most contracts that both parties have vetted and agreed to work with each other.

perturbation.justification: The changed text fundamentally alters the assignment clause. The original text requires consent from the other party for any transfer, assignment, or sublicense, while the modified text allows a party to transfer the agreement with consent only from itself and without even informing the counterparty, essentially nullifying the need for any mutual agreement, which is the purpose of the clause. This is a structural flaw that introduces a legal contradiction, reversing the protections of the clause.
